axioms.name: atyantam_atathabhute
axioms.cite: the ground being utterly otherwise (a single moving point)

axioms.name: drsyate
axioms.cite: vastvakara-nirupana drsyate: a thing-form is determined, seen, held

axioms.name: nirupayati
axioms.cite: it is vac, the word, that builds the circle out of the whirl

denials.name: parinama
denials.cite: Perception supplies moving light; the word supplies the cakra

claims.name: atyantam_atathabhute
claims.cite: the occasioning ground is utterly not so

claims.name: nirupayati
claims.cite: by resort to the utterance one sees the determination of a thing-form

rejected_readings.label: perception_produces_circle
rejected_readings.rendering: The whirling torch through perceptual contact naturally produces the circular form.
rejected_readings.why: Attributes form-production to perception (the alata/torch), contradicting the verse's explicit point that it is the word which supplies the cakra. Reverses the locus of determination from utterance to perceptual sensation.

rejected_readings.label: ground_sufficient_for_reality
rejected_readings.rendering: When a real and adequate ground exists, language determines how we apprehend the resulting object.
rejected_readings.why: Misses the verse's core teaching: it is precisely where the ground is NOT adequate (utterly otherwise) that language's independent form-giving power is most evident. This reading makes the word merely descriptive rather than constitutive of form.

rejected_readings.label: perception_mediates_form_manifestation
rejected_readings.rendering: Perception of the moving light directly enables or constitutes the manifestation of the circular form-determination.
rejected_readings.why: Misattributes to perception the form-constitutive function that the verse assigns to the word. The verse's structure (Perception supplies moving light; the word supplies the cakra) establishes that perception and word supply different aspects—perception does not supply the vastvakara, which is what the word alone accomplishes.

notes: Verse 1.129 demonstrates language's form-constitutive power through the fire-brand circle, where no real object exists yet a thing-form is nonetheless determined purely through utterance. The contract captures four core principles through its three axioms: (1) the ground (nimitte) is utterly otherwise, (2) the thing-form-determination IS seen/appears (drsyate), (3) the word (sabda) constitutively determines the thing-form, (4) perception supplies the moving light but the word supplies the circular form. The addition of the drsyate axiom captures the paradoxical resolution of the teaching—despite having no real substrate, the word-constituted form is genuinely manifested, not merely imagined. This exemplifies 1.128 by showing the extreme case where utterance alone furnishes object-form.
